data.title: ESALQ Citrus Analysis
data.description: citrus analysis CITRUS/CEPEA: Exporters get tariff relief; tahiti lime prices move down Cepea, 21 - The export sector of citrus products has gained an important tariff relief over the last days. The government from the United States has exempted the additional tariff of 10% for the orange juice at the end of last week. As for byproducts (essential oils, therapeutic byproducts and orange pulp), the 10%tariff continues, but they were exempted from the 40% tariff. This is good news for the sector, since it faces a slow pace of OJ shipments, especially to Europe. The European Union has been purchasing less than the usual. Therefore, the reduction of costs to send the product to the US market may favor sales of the Brazilian product, compensating the smaller demand from Europe. Partial data about 2025/26 exports indicate that, without the increase of US purchases, the performance would be even worse, since the North-American market has been currently purchasing higher volumes than Europe. TAHITI LIME - Prices are moving down in the domestic market, influenced by the high supply. The quality of fruits available is considered low, which also presses down values. The price average is at BRL 25.19 per 27.2-kg box this week, dropping 18.17% compared to the period before. Quotations of the tahiti lime to export have averaged BRL 30.81 per box, 13.39% down. For the Portuguese version please refer to or click on <ESQ/CIT/ANALYS>

data.title: PLATTS: 310--PJM Interconnection prices slide on Intercontinental Exchange - 21Nov25
data.description: (Platts)--21Nov2025/134 pm EST/1834 GMT On-peak power prices for Nov. 24 delivery in the PJM Interconnection footprint tumbled on the Intercontinental Exchange Nov. 21, despite an expected rise in demand. Two PJM hubs posted declines of roughly $13. AEP Dayton moved to $48/MWh, and West Hub fell to just below $52/MWh. West Hub's real-time peak number also dipped by $13, shifting its price to just above $51.50/MWh. PJM expected peakload to hit 99.3 MWh on Nov. 24, a 4.5% gain from its Nov. 21 expectation. CustomWeather anticipated average regional temperatures at 46.2 degrees Fahrenheit to open the workweek. Chicago, Philadelphia and Pittsburgh should all peak around 55 F on Nov. 24, according to the National Weather Service. The Midcontinent Independent System Operator's Indiana Hub posted a decline of about $2.50, dropping its price to $52/MWh. MISO forecast a 51% rise in Nov. 24 wind-power generation from its Nov. 21 expectation, but solar-power output was anticipated to drop 21% in that window. Average MISO regional temperatures to open the workweek stood at 45.2 F, according to CustomWeather, up 1.3 degrees in three days. Highs in Cincinnati and Indianapolis should hit the high 50s, the National Weather Service forecast. The Southwest Power Pool's South Hub day-ahead peak number moved to $40/MWh, up $1.50. The increase was fueled by an expected 5.2% rise in demand to 32.4 GWh from its Nov. 23 number. CustomWeather forecast SPP-area temperatures at a 55.7-degree average for Nov. 24, up 4.1 degrees in 72 hours. Wichita, Kansas, and Oklahoma City will open the workweek with highs in the 60s, according to the National Weather Service. — Michael Fox, michael.fox@spglobal.com-- Edited by Karina Roman, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: Cisco Co-Founder Sandy Lerner Lists Virginia Farm for $19.8 Million — WSJ
data.description:
By Katherine Clarke

When technology entrepreneur Sandy Lerner bought Virginia's Ayrshire Farm in 1996, the early-20th century manor house had been uninhabited for roughly a decade. The pipes had burst, the basement was flooded with water, and the ceiling's crown moldings were cracked and crumbling.

"The joke was that if you stood in one place long enough, something would hit you on the head," said Lerner, co-founder of the networking-equipment company Cisco Systems.

She paid $7 million for the roughly 800-acre farm outside Washington, D.C., and spent three years and tens of millions of dollars fixing it up. At the same time, she left Silicon Valley to become a full-time organic farmer. Ayrshire Farm, where she raises cattle, chickens and other livestock, was among the first in Virginia to achieve both Certified Humane and USDA Certified Organic status.

"Everybody says to me, 'Why did you move to Virginia?'" Lerner said. "My answer is, 'Because I could.'"

Now that she is slowing down at age 70, Lerner said, she is putting 571 acres of the farm on the market for $19.8 million.

Located in Upperville, about 60 miles outside of Washington, D.C., Ayrshire's stately manor house was built for Brigadier General James A. Buchanan, Lerner said. Fieldstone quarried on the estate was used to build the roughly 17,000-square-foot residence. When completed around 1911, she said, it had luxuries such as electricity, indoor plumbing and central heating. However, the house was left largely unused after 1987, when Buchanan's granddaughter died, according to the family.

For Lerner, purchasing Ayrshire marked a dramatic turning point in her life. After co-founding Cisco in 1984 with her then-husband, Leonard Bosack, she had made a name for herself in Silicon Valley as a powerful female tech entrepreneur. But she was fired from the company after it went public in 1990. For a while she stuck around the Valley, founding the cosmetics brand Urban Decay, which has since been acquired by L'Oréal.

But Lerner, who divorced Bosack in the early 1990s, felt unsettled despite her success. "I was sort of restless," she said.

Her solution was unorthodox: She left behind her high profile in business to focus on organic farming.

The decision wasn't as arbitrary it might initially seem. For one, Lerner had grown up in a ranching family in Northern California. "I've always been an animal nut," she said, recalling how she used to lie on the family's steers while doing her homework. She was active on the farm from age 6, she said, mucking out barns while her friends were "fixing their hair."

While at Cisco, she traveled frequently to trade shows on the East Coast and fell in love with the mid-Atlantic region.

She looked at close to 50 properties in the region before settling on Ayrshire, which she was advised to avoid because it was in such bad shape. She didn't listen. The naysayers "had the wrong vampire," she said.

Lerner started the Ayrshire renovation by restoring the stables on the property. Once an avid jouster at Renaissance fairs, she had come from California with four shire horses, and she wanted to make sure they were situated first. Meanwhile, she lived in a mouse-infested bedroom in the main house.

At the time, the 14-bedroom house was configured with a "strange warren of rooms," she said. She replaced a section of the house with a glass-walled conservatory shipped from England.

She also restored moldings and other original details; the library and butler's pantry retain their original working safes, for example.

While Lerner poured herself into restoring the manor house, her acquisition of Ayrshire was always more about farming. At the farm's peak, Lerner had about 2,000 head of cattle and was raising about 20,000 chickens each month. The animals aren't included in the sale price, but a buyer could potentially purchase them separately.

Lerner said she is selling Ayrshire because she is looking to simplify her life. While she used to live in the manor, in recent years she has spent most of her time in a smaller cabin on an adjacent 222-acre parcel of land, which she plans to keep.

"It's an easy house to live in, but it does get a little bit weird with just one person," she said of the larger manor.

Ayrshire is one of the most significant properties to come on the market in Virginia horse country in recent years, said listing agent Daniel Heider of TTR Sotheby's International Realty, who is co-listing the property with colleague Laura Farrell.

Lerner said she is hopeful that a prospective buyer might also take over the farming operations, though she admits it isn't lucrative.

"I had to go make money to support my farming habit," she said.

Write to Katherine Clarke at Katherine.Clarke@wsj.com

data.title: Google Just Dodged Its Biggest Threat Yet -- And AI May Have Handed It the Lifeline
data.description:
Google (GOOGL) just walked out of one of the biggest antitrust battles in years with surprisingly little damage, and the irony is striking: the same disastrous Bard rollout that once wiped 8% off Alphabet's value may have helped the company avoid deeper regulatory cuts. In 2024, Judge Amit Mehta ruled that Google illegally monopolized search, in part by paying billions of dollars to Apple and Samsung to secure its default status. But a year later, in his 230-page remedies decision, the judge concluded Google would be required to change almost nothing and he tied that outcome directly to the rise of generative AI. He argued that AI could be the new threat that introduces competition, turning what looked like a regulatory trapdoor into a possible long-term advantage for the company.

Inside the courtroom, big-tech leaders painted very different visions of the future. Microsoft's Satya Nadella testified about his fear that Google could extend its search dominance into AI, while Google's Prabhakar Raghavan described losing sleep over young users drifting away from what he called Grandpa Google. Yet scholars like Tim Wu and Cory Doctorow argue that this narrative of AI automatically toppling entrenched giants may be far too optimistic. Their upcoming books describe how monopolies tend to shift from innovation to extraction when competition fades, with Doctorow arguing that Google's search quality has declined for economic reasons an assertion Google firmly disputes. Both authors point to examples across Amazon, Apple and Unity as cases where platforms moved to increase fees and tighten control once market power was secured.

For investors, the broader tension sits inside the AI race now dominated by the Magnificent 7 Alphabet, Amazon , Apple , Meta , Microsoft, Nvidia and Tesla which represent roughly one-third of the S&P 500's value. Judge Mehta described the astonishing amount of capital pouring into AI and suggested limiting Google's distribution of Gemini could disadvantage it. But the supposed wave of new challengers isn't as independent as it appears: OpenAI relies on Microsoft, Anthropic is backed by Amazon and Google, Perplexity counts Jeff Bezos and Nvidia among its funders, and xAI is financed by Elon Musk. Even Apple is opting to integrate Google's AI into Siri rather than build its own. On Acquired, hosts Ben Gilbert and David Rosenthal argued that Google may ultimately outlast its AI rivals because it has fresher data, enormous compute, deep personalization and profits while many AI startups are burning cash with no net income. Whether AI becomes a tool that expands productivity or another force concentrating power may depend on how much genuine competition actually emerges, not how much disruption regulators hope will appear on its own.

data.title: Veeva Systems Earnings Beat Expectations. Why the Stock Is On Pace for Its Largest Drop Since 2023. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Veeva Systems raised its full-year outlook for the third quarter in a row but shares were sliding Friday as one detail from the earnings call overshadowed the software company's quarterly results.

Shares slumped 11% after Veeva said it expected to end up with just 14 of the largest 20 biopharmaceutical companies as Vault CRM customers, versus previous higher expectations, with rival Salesforce securing the other six.

Veeva broke off its partnership with Salesforce in 2022 and released Vault CRM, its customer relationship management platform, two years later to replace software it had built using Salesforce technology. Mizuho analysts remarked Friday that this "win-loss ratio" likely would overshadow an encouraging fiscal-year guidance increase.

Veeva said it now expects fiscal-year adjusted earnings of roughly $7.93 a share, up from $7.78 and above the $7.90 Wall Street had forecast. The company also guided for $3.17 billion in revenue, versus a prior range of $3.13 billion to $3.14 billion. Analysts were looking for $3.16 billion.

Fiscal third-quarter earnings also broadly beat expectations. Veeva posted adjusted earnings of $2.04 a share for the period ended Oct. 31, surpassing the $1.95 analysts had anticipated, according to FactSet. Revenue was $811.2 million, topping calls for $792.8 million.

Friday's declines put the stock on pace for its worst same-day percentage decrease since a 14% plunge on Nov. 9, 2023, according to Dow Jones Market Data. Shares have risen 13% this year, outstripping an 11% gain for the benchmark S&P 500.

Transaction Summary

The certificates are supported by 523 loans with a total balance of approximately $661.30 million as of the cutoff date. The pool consists of prime jumbo fixed-rate mortgages acquired by Redwood Residential Acquisition Corp. (RRAC) from Rocket Mortgage and various mortgage originators. Distributions of principal and interest (P&I) and loss allocations are based on a senior-subordinate, shifting-interest structure, with full advancing.

The borrowers in the pool exhibit a strong credit profile, with a weighted-average (WA) Fitch FICO of 773 and 37.0% debt-to-income (DTI) ratio. The borrowers also have moderate leverage, with a 72.9% mark-to-market combined LTV (cLTV). Overall, 93.1% of the pool loans are for primary residences, while the remainder are second homes or investment properties. Additionally, 100% of the loans were underwritten to full documentation.

Following the publication of Fitch's expected ratings, the issuer provided an updated collateral tape that included two loan drops along with an updated structure in which Class A-30 was renamed as Class A-CH4. Fitch re-ran its asset and cashflow analysis and confirmed this had no effect on its expected ratings.

KEY RATING DRIVERS

Credit Risk of Mortgage Assets: RMBS transactions are directly affected by the performance of the underlying residential mortgages or mortgage-related assets. Fitch analyzes loan-level attributes and macroeconomic factors to assess the credit risk and expected losses. SEMT 2025-12 has a final probability of default (PD) of 10.05% in the 'AAAsf' rating stress. Fitch's final loss severity in the 'AAAsf' rating stress is 35.96%. The final loss in the 'AAAsf' rating stress is 3.61%.

Structural Analysis: The mortgage cash flow and loss allocation in SEMT 2025-12 are based on a senior-subordinate, shifting-interest structure, whereby the subordinate classes receive only scheduled principal and are locked out from receiving unscheduled principal or prepayments for five years.

Fitch analyses the capital structure to determine the adequacy of the transaction's credit enhancement (CE) to support payments on the securities under multiple scenarios incorporating Fitch's loss projections derived from the asset analysis. Fitch applies its assumptions for defaults, prepayments, delinquencies and interest rate scenarios. The CE for all ratings were sufficient for the given rating levels. The credit CE or a given rating exceeded the expected losses of that rating stress to address the structures recoupment of advances and leakage of principal to more subordinate classes.

Operational Risk Analysis: Fitch considers originator and servicer capability, third-party due diligence results, and the transaction-specific representation, warranty and enforcement (RW&E) framework to derive a potential operational risk adjustment. The only consideration that has a direct impact on Fitch's loss expectations is due diligence. Third-party due diligence was performed on 97.7% of the loans in the transaction by loan count. Fitch applies a 5bp z-score reduction for loans fully reviewed by a third-party review (TPR) firm, which have a final grade of either "A" or "B."

Counterparty and Legal Analysis: Fitch expects all relevant transaction parties to conform with the requirements described in its Global Structured Finance Rating Criteria. Relevant parties are those whose failure to perform could have a material impact on the performance of the transaction. Additionally, all legal requirements should be satisfied to fully de-link the transaction from any other entities. SEMT 2025-12 is fully de-linked and a bankruptcy remote special purpose vehicle (SPV). All transaction parties and triggers align with Fitch's expectations.

Rating Cap Analysis: Common rating caps in U.S. RMBS may include, but are not limited to, new product types with limited or volatile historical data and transactions with weak operational or structural/counterparty features. These considerations do not apply to SEMT 2025-12, and therefore, Fitch is comfortable assigning the highest possible rating of 'AAAsf' without any rating caps.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

Fitch incorporates a sensitivity analysis to demonstrate how the ratings would react to steeper market value declines (MVDs) than assumed at the metropolitan statistical area level. Sensitivity analysis was conducted at the state and national levels to assess the effect of higher MVDs for the subject pool as well as lower MVDs, illustrated by a gain in home prices.

The defined negative rating sensitivity analysis demonstrates how the ratings would react to steeper MVDs at the national level. The analysis assumes MVDs of 10%, 20% and 30%, in addition to the model-projected 37.6% at 'AAAsf'. The analysis indicates there is some potential rating migration with higher MVDs compared to the model projection. Specifically, a 10% additional decline in home prices would lower all rated classes by one full category.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

Fitch incorporates a sensitivity analysis to demonstrate how the ratings would react to steeper MVDs than assumed at the MSA level. Sensitivity analysis was conducted at the state and national levels to assess the effect of higher MVDs for the subject pool as well as lower MVDs, illustrated by a gain in home prices.

This defined positive rating sensitivity analysis demonstrates how the ratings would react to positive home price growth of 10% with no assumed overvaluation. Excluding the senior class, which is already rated 'AAAsf', the analysis indicates there is potential positive rating migration for all the rated classes. Specifically, a 10% gain in home prices would result in a full category upgrade for the rated class, excluding those assigned ratings of 'AAAsf'.

USE OF THIRD PARTY DUE DILIGENCE PURSUANT TO SEC RULE 17G -10

Fitch was provided with Form ABS Due Diligence-15E (Form 15E) as prepared by SitusAMC, Clayton, and Consolidated Analytics. The third-party due diligence described in Form 15E focused on credit, compliance, and property valuation. Fitch considered this information in its analysis and, as a result, Fitch applied an approximate 5-bp z-score reduction for loans fully reviewed by the TPR firm and have a final grade of "A" or "B."

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. REPRESENTATIONS, WARRANTIES AND ENFORCEMENT MECHANISMS A description of the transaction's representations, warranties and enforcement mechanisms (RW&Es) that are disclosed in the offering document and which relate to the underlying asset pool is available by clicking the link to the Appendix. The appendix also contains a comparison of these RW&Es to those Fitch considers typical for the asset class as detailed in the Special Report titled 'Representations, Warranties and Enforcement Mechanisms in Global Structured Finance Transactions'.

ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit https://www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: Fitch Removes Blue Owl Capital Corp. II's Rating Watch Positive; Affirms at 'BBB-'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-21 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed Blue Owl Capital Corporation II's (OBDC II) Long-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDR) and senior unsecured debt rating at 'BBB-' and the senior secured debt rating at 'BBB'. The ratings have been removed from Rating Watch Positive (RWP) and subsequently the Rating Outlook is Stable. This action follows the announcement that the proposed merger of OBDC II and Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC; BBB/Stable) has been terminated. OBDC's 'BBB' rating and Stable Outlook are unaffected by these actions.

Key Rating Drivers

Merger Termination Drives Removal of Positive Watch: OBDC II's ratings were placed on RWP following the announcement of a planned merger with OBDC, reflecting Fitch's view that the merger would have positive rating implications for OBDC II's ratings given OBDC's more favorable credit profile. Following OBDC and OBDC II announcing a termination of the merger on Nov. 19, 2025, Fitch is removing the ratings from RWP.

Platform Affiliation Benefits: The rating affirmation reflects OBDC II's access to investment resources from Blue Owl Capital Inc. (Blue Owl), an experienced management team, above-average underlying portfolio company EBITDA, below-average leverage, sufficient funding flexibility and adequate liquidity. The ratings also reflect the solid asset quality since inception, although non-accruals increased in 3Q25 amounting to 4.5% of debt investments at cost and 2.2% at fair value.

Rating Constraints: The rating is constrained by the potential for elevated tender requests following the termination of the proposed merger, which could negatively affect leverage and liquidity. Tenders peaked at 5.9% of net asset value in 3Q25 and averaged 4.7% quarterly in 1Q25-3Q25, which are above historical levels. The tender program was terminated in connection with the merger announcement, but the firm plans to reinstate the program in 1Q26, subject to board approval.

Additional rating constraints include the inability for OBDC II to access the equity markets for capital barring a liquidity event, below-average asset coverage cushion, above-average exposure to payment-in-kind (PIK) income, a recent decline in dividend coverage and an increase in unsecured debt maturities in 2026. The size of the Blue Owl business development company (BDC) platform may also limit capital markets' capacity to provide the BDCs with sufficient unsecured financing.

Below Average Asset Coverage: At Sept. 30, 2025, OBDC II's leverage (par debt to equity) was 0.83x, or 0.78x net of cash, above the firm's target of 0.75x. Gross leverage implied an asset coverage cushion of 9.5%, below Fitch's 'bbb' category leverage benchmark range of 11%-33%, although it would improve to 12.7% if cash were utilized to pay down debt. Fitch believes OBDC II's asset coverage cushion will remain below the rated peer average since the firm is the only rated BDC subject to a 200% asset coverage requirement but expects the firm will seek to utilize cash flows from repayments to manage leverage near the targeted level. A sustained decline in the asset coverage cushion could drive negative rating momentum.

BDC Sector Constraints: Rating constraints for BDCs include the market impact on leverage, dependence on access to the capital markets to fund growth and limited ability to retain capital. Fitch believes BDCs will continue to face a competitive environment, weaker earnings and dividend coverage metrics, and pressure on asset quality metrics in 2026.

Stable Outlook: The Stable Outlook reflects Fitch's expectations for the maintenance of solid asset quality metrics, an improvement in asset coverage cushion to at least 11%, and the maintenance of sufficient liquidity relative to unfunded commitments, potential redemption obligations and near-term debt maturities.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--A sustained decline in the asset coverage cushion below 11%;

--Deterioration of the portfolio risk profile, such that first lien positions declined materially as a proportion of the overall portfolio, without a commensurate decline in leverage;

--A sustained meaningful increase in non-accrual levels and/or meaningful realized or unrealized losses;

--A sustained decline in unsecured debt below 35% of total debt outstanding;

--Weaker dividend coverage;

--An increase in the proportion of PIK income without a demonstrated ability to collect accrued PIK in cash or an impairment of the firm's liquidity profile;

--An inability to access the public equity markets for an extended period would also be viewed negatively.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--Fitch views positive rating momentum as unlikely in the near term given OBDC II's lack of access to the public equity markets barring a liquidity event;

--However, if OBDC II gained access to the public equity markets a sustained improvement in the asset coverage cushion and solid credit performance, evaluated in combination with the consistency of OBDC II's operating performance, asset quality metrics, investment valuations and underlying portfolio metrics, could lead to positive rating momentum.

--Positive rating momentum would also be conditioned upon the maintenance of sufficient liquidity, the maintenance of unsecured debt above 40% of total debt outstanding and solid cash-based NII coverage of the dividend.

DEBT AND OTHER INSTRUMENT RATINGS: KEY RATING DRIVERS

The senior secured debt rating, which is one notch above OBDC II's Long-Term IDR, reflects Fitch's view of good recovery prospects in a stress scenario given its funding mix and available asset coverage.

The alignment of the unsecured debt rating with the Long-Term IDR reflects average recovery prospects under a stress scenario given that OBDC II is subject to a 200% regulatory asset coverage requirement.

DEBT AND OTHER INSTRUMENT RATINGS: RATING SENSITIVITIES

The secured and unsecured debt ratings are primarily linked to the Long-Term IDR and are expected to move in tandem with it. However, the notching could change if there is a shift in the funding mix or reduction in available asset coverage that Fitch believes affects the recovery prospects of the instruments in a stress scenario.

ADJUSTMENTS

The Standalone Credit Profile (SCP) has been assigned in line with the implied SCP.

data.title: Novo Nordisk Patients in Denmark Receive Compensation for Vision Loss Linked to Wegovy and Ozempic
data.description:
Novo Nordisk (NVO, Financials) saw renewed attention after Denmark's patient compensation authority approved payouts to four individuals who experienced vision loss while taking semaglutide-based drugs Wegovy and Ozempic. The patients were awarded a total of 800,000 Danish crowns following a specialist review. One additional claim was rejected.

The authority said the cases were complex because semaglutide has been linked in very rare situations to non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, a condition that can cause permanent vision loss. European regulators added NAION to product labels in June, noting it may occur in up to 1 in 10,000 patients.

Denmark's compensation authority, which operates independently and is funded by the state, has received 43 claims related to alleged semaglutide-associated vision issues. The rulings do not assign legal liability to Novo Nordisk.

Semaglutide remains central to Novo Nordisk's diabetes and weight-management portfolio, and demand for both medications remains elevated.

data.title: Ozempic Vision Risk Sparks Payouts: Novo Nordisk's Weight-Loss Empire Faces First Legal Blowback
data.description:
The obesity-drug boom just ran into a harder edge in Novo Nordisk's home market. Danish authorities have now granted 800,000 kroner in total compensation to four patients who say they developed NAIONa rare, irreversible eye conditionafter using the company's semaglutide drugs. The state-backed body overseeing claims has received 43 in total, ruled on five, and approved four, while emphasizing that eligibility depends on the specifics of each case. For investors riding the Wegovy-Ozempic wave, the development could be a reminder that explosive demand often brings parallel scrutiny.

NAION is uncommon but serious, driven by a loss of blood flow to the optic nerve and often striking people over 50. Denmark's compensation chief described it as a disease that causes permanent and incurable damage to visiona message that may prompt more patients to come forward. Novo Nordisk said it takes all adverse-event reports seriously and has already updated patient leaflets to reflect NAION as a very rare adverse drug reaction, though it still believes the benefit-risk profile of semaglutide could remain favorable across approved uses.

The regulatory context adds another layer. Earlier this year, the European Medicines Agency concluded that NAION could affect as many as 1 in 10,000 people taking semaglutide medicines, following Denmark's request to evaluate two independent studies showing that people with type 2 diabetes on Ozempic may face more than twice the risk. The first suggestion of a link surfaced in 2024 in a Harvard University study, setting the stage for today's heightened oversight. For investors, the picture could be shifting from a pure growth narrative toward a more complicated balance of blockbuster demand and evolving safety signals.

data.title: HSBC in Crisis Mode? Swiss Wealth Unit Bleeds Talent, Slashes Ties with Billionaires
data.description:
HSBC's Swiss private bank is navigating one of its most delicate moments in years, and the response is starting to show. After months of departures including interim head John Shipman, who left last month for Barclays Plc the bank is quietly lifting compensation for select staff in what could be a defensive move to keep competitors from poaching talent. People familiar with the matter said the pay boosts include relationship managers, a group that often becomes the first target when wealth units hit turbulence.

Behind the scenes, HSBC is pulling in senior reinforcements. Samir Assaf, the chairman of its Middle East business and an adviser to the bank's leadership, has been spending time in Geneva to help steady the operation. The bank, which has said its strategy is to significantly grow its Wealth business, recently promoted Daniel Calado to lead the Swiss unit on an ad interim basis after Shipman's exit. All of this comes as the Swiss arm is in the process of ending relationships with more than 1,000 wealthy Middle Eastern clients some with over $100 million in assets according to earlier Bloomberg reporting, a shift that could be reshaping the entire franchise.

Regulatory pressure is also building around the edges. The Swiss watchdog Finma previously found due-diligence lapses on high-risk accounts tied to politically exposed persons, and Swiss federal prosecutors have opened a probe into the division. In moments like these, retention packages often become a stabilizing tool the same playbook UBS used when it handed out about $500 million to key Credit Suisse staff after announcing their merger. HSBC's version could be an attempt to safeguard its bench while navigating a revamp that may still have several moving parts.
